sources.notes: For each prefix ending position, maximize subarray weight where weight counts elements greater than the subarray MEX.
Official hints enumerate the missing value x, choose l after the last occurrence of x, and maintain all candidate x values with a segment tree.

annotation.summary: For every right endpoint, find the maximum subarray weight, where weight is the number of elements greater than the subarray MEX.
annotation.constraints: n is up to 3e5, so all possible MEX candidates must be maintained together while sweeping the right endpoint.
annotation.core_idea: For a candidate missing value x, among subarrays ending at r that do not contain x, the best left endpoint is immediately after the last occurrence of x. The score for x is then the number of elements greater than x since that last occurrence. Sweep r from left to right: the new value a[r] increases the score of all x<a[r], while x=a[r] stops being absent and its candidate score is reset to reflect the new last occurrence. A lazy segment tree stores all candidate scores and returns their maximum.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) per test case.
tricks: last occurrence defines best left endpoint

solution_variants.name: candidate-mex-segment-tree
solution_variants.summary: Sweep the right endpoint and maintain each possible missing value's best score with range add and point reset.

tags.evidence: The solution needs lazy range increments over candidate MEX values and global maximum queries.
Each new array element contributes +1 to a whole range of candidate missing values, while its own candidate is reset.
The best left boundary for a candidate x is tracked by the moving last occurrence of x.
